<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:畿内・近国の戦国時代]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:畿内・近国の戦国時代]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:畿内・近国の戦国時代]]


{{Expand Japanese}}
'''The Sengoku Period of Kinai and Neighboring Provinces''' (畿内・近国の戦国時代, *Kinai-Kingoku no Sengoku Jidai*) covers the Sengoku period of {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|WD=Q906708|lt=Kinai|1=Kinai}} and its neighboring provinces, roughly corresponding to the modern {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} during the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} and the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]].

There are various theories regarding the chronological scope of the Sengoku period. This article follows the convention that defines its beginning with the [[Meiō Coup]] of {{ill|Meiō|en|Meiō|WD=Q1193841|lt=Meiō|1=Meiō}} 2 (1493)<ref>Toriumi Toshiharu & [[Yumikura Hironori]], eds., ''The Sengoku Period of Southern Kinai'', [[Ebisukōshō Publishing]], 2017, p.1.</ref> and its end with the fall of the Muromachi shogunate in {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 1 (1573).<ref>Arimitsu Tomonori, ed., ''The Regional States of the Sengoku Period'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2003, p.71.</ref>

== Overview==
{{Double image aside|right|洛中洛外図左.jpg|300|洛中洛外図右.jpg|300|Scenes of Kyoto during the Eiroku era, from the [[Uesugi version of the Rakuchū Rakugai-zu]].}} 
{{After float}}

During the Sengoku period, Kinai was home to the capital city of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, where the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}} ruled under the authority of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}, with the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan|1=Hosokawa clan}} serving as *{{ill|Kanrei|en|Kanrei|WD=Q1202497|lt=Kanrei|1=Kanrei}}* (Shogunal Deputy). Kinai consisted of five provinces: {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro|1=Yamashiro Province}}, home to Kyoto; {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato Province}}, where the [[Kōfuku-ji]] temple held de facto {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}} authority despite the absence of an official protector; {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu|1=Settsu Province}}, where the {{ill|Hongan-ji|en|Hongan-ji|WD=Q1627016|lt=Hongan-ji|1=Hongan-ji}} sect established its headquarters in Osaka during the [[Tenbun (era)|Tenbun era]]; {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|WD=Q1047117|lt=Izumi|1=Izumi Province}}, home to the international trading port of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}}; and {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi|1=Kawachi Province}}, governed by the {{ill|Hatakeyama clan|en|Hatakeyama clan|WD=Q662881|lt=Hatakeyama clan|1=Hatakeyama clan}}, one of the {{ill|Kanrei|en|Kanrei|WD=Q1202497|lt=Three *Kanrei* Clans|1=Kanrei}}. These five provinces were collectively referred to as "*Tenka*" (天下, "the realm").<ref>[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASN8225ZTN76PTJB010.html?iref=com_rnavi_aRank_nr05 'Was the Fall of Nobunaga’s Regime Due to Its Contradictions? Three New Theories Emerge'] - {{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}}</ref> Additionally, nearby regions such as {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}}, which contained many {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial court|1=Imperial Court in Kyoto}} and [[Kuge|noble]] estates, and {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi|1=Ōmi Province}}, controlled by the {{ill|Rokkaku clan|en|Rokkaku clan|WD=Q2975342|lt=Rokkaku clan|1=Rokkaku clan}}, where the Ashikaga shoguns frequently sought refuge from conflicts in Kyoto, are also covered in this section.{{Efn|According to the ''Chronicle Summary'' (*Nendaiki Shōsetsu*), the scope of "*Tenka*" in *Eiroku* 11 (1568) included nine provinces: the five Kinai provinces plus [[Harima Province|Harima]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kuno Masashi |date=2020-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.34428/00013855 |title=The Kinai Rule of the Oda Nobunaga Regime |journal=Hakusan Shigaku |ISSN=0385-9460 |publisher=Hakusan Historical Society |volume=56 |pages=65-105 |doi=10.34428/00013855 |naid=40022207202 |CRID=1390294573746178432 |ref={{harvid|Kuno Masashi|2020b}}}} p.95</ref>.}}

The political landscape of Kinai and its neighboring provinces during the Sengoku period was marked by the spread of the conflict between {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshitane|en|Ashikaga Yoshitane|WD=Q708527|lt=Ashikaga Yoshitane|1=Ashikaga Yoshitane}} and {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshizumi|en|Ashikaga Yoshizumi|WD=Q466281|lt=Ashikaga Yoshizumi|1=Ashikaga Yoshizumi}} in the Meiō-Eishō era,<ref>Matsushima Shūichi, "[https://hdl.handle.net/10424/4509 The Imagawa Clan and Western Mikawa in the Eishō Era]", ''Nihon Bunka Ronso'', Vol. 20, Aichi University, 2012, pp. 97-99, {{hdl|10424/4509}}</ref> and the ongoing struggle between the {{ill|Miyoshi clan|en|Miyoshi clan|WD=Q2975329|lt=Miyoshi clan|1=Miyoshi clan}} and the shogunate during the Eiroku era, which created new political structures in the provinces.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Furuno Mitsugu, Murai Ryōsuke, Fujimoto Takahiro, Amano Tadayuki, Toriumi Toshiharu, Nishijima Tarō, Fujimoto Fumiko |date=2010-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.24544/ocu.20171213-107 |title=Reconstructing the Study of Kinai in the Sengoku Period and the 'Hosokawa Ryoke-ki' |journal=Urban Culture Research |ISSN=1348-3293 |publisher=Osaka City University Graduate School of Literature: Urban Culture Research Center |volume=12 |pages=159-178 |doi=10.24544/ocu.20171213-107 |naid=120006006922 |CRID=1390290699896202112}}</ref> These developments influenced not only Kinai and its neighboring provinces but also political movements across the entire Japanese archipelago.<ref>{{Cite |和書|author=Toriumi Toshiharu |date=2010-03 |url=https://www.lit.osaka-cu.ac.jp/UCRC/wp-content/uploads/2010/03/p159.pdf |format=PDF |title=Symposium: Reconstructing the Study of Kinai in the Sengoku Period and the 'Hosokawa Ryoke-ki' |chapter="Re-examining the Political History of the Sengoku Kinai through the Hatakeyama Clan" |journal=Urban Culture Research |ISSN=13483293 |publisher=Osaka City University Graduate School of Literature Urban Culture Research Center |issue=12 |pages=166-167 |CRID=1520853832233232128}}</ref> 

At the same time, regional [[Sengoku daimyō]] needed recognition from higher authorities to legitimize their rule, making connections with the Kyoto-based shogunate and Imperial Court indispensable.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Kobayashi Takehiko |date=1990-03 |url=https://glim-re.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/1533 |title=The Ōuchi Clan’s Policy Toward Kyoto: Focusing on the Role of Resident Agents (Monks) |journal=Gakushūin Shigaku |ISSN=02861658 |publisher=Gakushūin University Historical Society |issue=28 |page=17 |hdl=10959/1810 |CRID=1050564288171432704}}</ref><ref name=Imaoka>{{Cite journal|和書|author=Imaoka Norikazu |date=2009-03 |url=https://kusw.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/10 |title=Shugo Authority During the Sengoku Period: The Case of the Asakura Clan in Echizen |journal=Kansai Welfare University Bulletin of Social Welfare Studies |ISSN=1883566X |publisher=Kansai Welfare University |issue=12 |page=59 |naid=40016560024 |CRID=1050282812568651520}}</ref> Additionally, Kyoto often became a battleground for regional daimyō disputes, such as in the [[Yōkyūkai Incident]], highlighting the pivotal role of Kinai and its surrounding provinces in shaping the broader history of Sengoku Japan.

== Related articles ==
* {{ill|Moated settlements|en|Moated settlements|WD=Q60995739|lt=Moated settlement|1=Moated settlements}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=Sogo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}}
* {{ill|Holy city|en|Holy city|WD=Q692581|lt=Holy city|1=Holy city}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Monk-soldier|1=Sōhei}}
* {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}}
* {{ill|Moated settlements|en|Moated settlements|WD=Q60995739|lt=Enclosed settlements|1=Moated settlements}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}}
* {{ill|Holy city|en|Holy city|WD=Q692581|lt=Holy city|1=Holy city}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=寺社勢力|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine power|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}/{{ill|10=ja|11=アジール|Asylum|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=asylum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26377}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}
* {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Warrior monks|1=Sōhei}}
* {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=Kinai and nearby regions during the Warring States period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}

[[File:Isshinden Jinaicho.JPG|thumb|300px|Jinaicho formed around the main temple of Jōdo Shinshū Takada sect, Senshūji]]
[[File:Kyuhoji, dorui-ato.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Moats that demarcated Jinaicho<br />(Kyuhoji Jinaicho in Yao City, Osaka Prefecture)]]
*

<references />



<!--The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki--><!--The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki-->
{{translated page|ja|畿内・近国の戦国時代|version=104738173|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
[[ja:畿内・近国の戦国時代]]


{{wikidata link|Q55526369}}
[[Category:History of Japan by period]]
[[Category:History of the Kansai region]]
[[Category:Japanese history timelines]]
[[Category:Muromachi period]]
[[Category:Sengoku period]]
[[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
